Thanks for the comment po. I hope you dont mind if I turn it into a post. 

Quick thing though—I think what you mean here is prosecution, not persecution. 

Persecution is when you're being targeted and mistreated because of who you are. 

Prosecution is when someone gets charged and has to answer for what they did in court. 

Different things. We're not persecuting Sara. We want her to face prosecution—to account for the charges.

Your point about immunity is fair. 

A sitting VP does have legal protection. But impeachment is its own thing—it's literally designed to address high crimes while someone's in office. 

(That’s why people like Chiz Escudero and the Tsinators are doing everything in their power to stop it from happening.)

When the Supreme Court threw out those impeachment complaints last year, it wasn't saying Sara didn't do anything wrong. The court said the process was flawed procedurally. That's not the same as exoneration.

But here's the bigger problem: the Supreme Court overstepped. Hard.

The court didn't just rule on whether the impeachment followed constitutional timelines. 

It rewrote the rules. It changed what counts as a "session day." It created new definitions for when impeachment is considered "initiated." It added due process requirements that aren't in the Constitution. 

Leila de Lima called it judicial legislation. Tito Sotto called it an impossible dream now—impeachment itself. 

Because the court didn't interpret the law. It made new law.

That's judicial overreach. 

The Constitution gives Congress—not the Supreme Court—the exclusive power to initiate impeachment. That's separation of powers. 

But the SC stepped in and decided it could redefine how Congress runs its own process. 

Even lawmakers who were part of the prosecution said they'll comply with the ruling, but that doesn't mean they agree. 

They're talking about amending the Constitution now because the court moved the goalposts mid-game.

Now, your scenario about 2028. 

You're right that it's dangerous. If she becomes president, she could replace the ombudsman and the Sandiganbayan justices with her own appointees. 

That's the real vicious cycle you're naming—she gets to staff the very institutions meant to hold her accountable. 

That's a legitimate concern, and it's not paranoia. It's how power works in systems like ours.

But here's the thing: even then, the cases don't disappear. They sit in limbo while she's president, yeah. 

But when her term ends—whenever that is—those cases can move forward. 

Unless she finds a way to pardon herself or change the laws entirely. 

Which, in fairness, is not impossible. But that's a different conversation about how we've let executive power grow unchecked.

The real problem you're naming—that powerful people seem to slip through—that's legitimate. 

But here's what we don't talk about often enough: we as Filipinos often don't actually care if the politician we want is corrupt or a thief. 

We don't care if they run for office using stolen funds from the people. 

That's how we've been as a nation for the longest time. We pick our person and the rest becomes noise.

I'm glad you asked this question though, because not enough people sit with their own role in all this. 

We blame the system, blame the courts, blame immunity laws. But we don't internalize what we're actually doing when we look away from corruption because someone's on our side.

That's why I write the way I do. 

Even if only three people who read MCT actually internalize what's here and change how they think about it—I'm happy. 

Of course that's not the only goal. 

I'm hoping to reach people who can talk to other people, who can talk to more people after that. 

MCT is just a tool for the interested, willing, and able. 

I write in detail so when you're having coffee or dinner with someone, and the conversation veers into politics, you're armed with the facts and sources you need to actually explain things. To correct. To redirect. To push back. To make someone think twice.

That ripple matters more than the noise.

Magandang umaga po. Kape tayo.

“Hi MCT. I just wanted to ask: Why are you not talking to the mass market? Why do you write for people na nakaka intindi na?”

Good question. And it deserves a serious answer.

When people say “mass market,” usually ang ibig sabihin nila: the poor, the less educated, the busy, the exhausted, the ones who don’t spend their free time reading long political blogs. 

The uncomfortable truth in the Philippines is this: most of them don’t. 

They are on Facebook, TikTok, YouTube, TV, radio. They consume short, emotional, visual content, not 3,000-word breakdowns of laws, dynasties, and disinformation networks.

So if the expectation is that a single writer, on a personal page, can “talk to the mass market” by just switching to Filipino or shortening posts, that’s not how the ecosystem works. 

Even TV made “for the poor” often turns their suffering into spectacle more than education. 

That’s the information environment we’re all swimming in, like it or not.

This page was never meant to be a variety show or a meme factory. It is research-heavy on purpose.  

I read, I dig, I cross-check, I look at patterns: disinformation networks, economic decisions, political behavior, and how they affect everyday life.

The posts are long and data-heavy because I assume at least some of you want to be equipped, not just entertained. 

That is the lane I serve: depth, context, receipts.

And here’s the part I need to say plainly:  

If the goal is to “educate the masses,” a blog will never be enough. Not mine, not anyone’s.

The most powerful channels for belief formation in our context are still interpersonal: family conversations, church spaces, barangay gatherings, work chats, Viber and Messenger GCs, kapitbahay kwentuhan.

Face-to-face, or at least relationship-based sharing, is still the strongest way to influence people, especially those who don’t read long-form content.

That is why I keep saying:  

The main way to reach the so-called mass market is not to make them read blogs.  

The main way is for you—the one who reads—to bring what you learned into your own circles.

So when someone asks, “Why are you not talking to the mass market?” ang dating niyan parang ganito: 

“Since you started this, ikaw na bahala sa lahat. Saluhin mo na ang buong responsibility.”  

That is not realistic, and it quietly removes the reader (you) from the equation.

This page is a tool.  

What I offer you here is raw material: research, language, context, patterns, questions.

You don’t have to agree with everything, but you now have more to work with than memes and forwarded fake news. 

The subliminal goal has always been to replicate the advocacy, not hoard it. 

I do the digging so you don’t have to start from zero every time you talk to your parents, cousins, co-workers, church mates, drivers, or neighbors.

If you care about the masa, then you are part of the bridge.  

You read here.  
You process.  
Then you translate—in your own words, in your own style, in your own relationships.

I will keep writing long, detailed, data-heavy blogs, because that is how I can contribute best. 

I’m not going to pretend that dumbing everything down into catchy lines will magically awaken political awareness in a country drowning in disinformation and distraction.

But I also refuse the framing where all the responsibility sits on one page and one person.

So to answer the question directly:  

I am talking to you.

Because you are the one who reads.  

And if this advocacy is going to reach the people you call “mass market,” it will not be because of one blogger.  

It will be because readers like you decided to stop being just an audience and started becoming carriers.

₱1.5 million break bonus daw.

What does that even mean? And why are we all acting like this is fine?

1. Let’s start with the ₱1.5M

Batangas Rep. Leandro Leviste says that on the day the Lower House was supposed to vote on the 2026 budget in October 2025, 1.5 million pesos was being given out to lawmakers.  

Same day as a crucial budget vote, same place, and money is moving quietly through the building for congressmen.

He also says lawmakers do not just get regular monthly funds. There are extra amounts in October and December, conveniently timed with budget approval moments.  

So that 1.5 million pesos people are talking about is one of those “break” payouts during the Undas or budget period, separate from the later December “Christmas bonus” talk.

And the more you sit with that, the dirtier it feels.

2. Then Toby Tiangco drops the real bomb

Navotas Rep. Toby Tiangco comes in and basically says: yes, this happens, and it has been happening for years.

He admits that every time the Lower House goes on break, lawmakers receive what he calls a break bonus or allowance.  

He even says this has been going on since at least 2010, when he first became a congressman. This is not a new thing under the current administration. This is standard Lower House behavior.

He explains the pattern like this:

- Lawmakers get monthly MOOE, the usual office funds.  

- On top of that, they get extra money when the House goes on recess: Undas, Christmas, Holy Week, and other breaks.  

- He openly confirms he received 1.5 million pesos for the Undas break.

And the process? They sign a voucher, they get the checks, and these particular break amounts do not require detailed liquidation.

That is not an accident. That is a system designed to move money without real scrutiny.

3. Then there’s the 2 million “not-a-bonus” bonus

Leviste also talks about another amount: around 2 million pesos per lawmaker in December, after the bicam report on the 2026 budget was ratified. He calls it a Christmas bonus for some House members.

The response from House leaders is almost insulting:

- They say this is not a Christmas bonus, but MOOE, office money, standard allocation.  

- They insist the funds are for district activities, Christmas events, consultations, and programs.  

- Others claim the checks seen in photos are meant for staff salaries and operations, not a bonus for lawmakers.

So they play this word game:

- 1.5 million in October? Break allowance.  

- 2 million in December? Office funds only, definitely not a bonus.  

- Yet both are sent in checks, both are handled inside the same opaque internal budget, and both sit on top of an already bloated MOOE.

They keep insisting it is legal. Maybe it is. That is the point. They turned legal structures into tools to quietly reward themselves.

4. This is what “legal corruption” looks like

Look at the pattern and tell me this is not theft with paperwork.

- The Lower House’s internal budget jumps by 10.5 billion pesos, up to 27.7 billion, with no clear public explanation.  

- MOOE alone hits 18.58 billion, with a huge chunk listed as “other maintenance and operating expenses,” vague and loaded, often liquidated by certification instead of real receipts.  

- Extra money drops in October and December, in the middle of and right after major budget decisions.  

- A staffer even shows Leviste a 151 million peso package and calls it his incentive before a vote.

They are not just breaking rules. They are writing the rules in a way that lets them grab more while still being able to say, “It’s allowed. It’s audited. It’s office funds.”

That is why it stings: they can say it is legal while everyone can see it is abusive.  
It is legal corruption, dressed up as MOOE, liquidations by certification, and break allowances.

And the worst part? They know most people will shrug and move on.

5. The part that really makes me angry

Tiangco saying this has been happening since 2010 is supposed to calm us down, like: relax, this is normal, this is how the Lower House works.  
For me, that makes it worse.

Because if this has been the habit for more than a decade, that means:

- We have been funding these break bonuses for years without being told they exist.  

- They are so used to it that they talk about 1.5 million and 2 million like it is just another day at work.  

- We are now at a point where they can openly admit it on TV and still expect nothing to change.

They keep finding new labels, new excuses, new categories in the budget. Office funds. Programs. For the district.  
We keep paying for it through taxes, higher prices, and broken services.

And most of us are so numb we are not even angry anymore. We just swallow it.  

We share a link. 
We rant for one night. 
Then we move on.

Meanwhile, they stay. They sign another voucher. Another check. Another break. Another not-a-bonus.

All legal.

And now, after staring at this mess in the Lower House, there is one more question I cannot shake:

If this is what is happening here,  
what does it look like on the Senate side?

The other question: how come I am hearing about this from Tiangco and Leviste, congressmen I gave zero trust to?

How come I am not hearing about it, loudly and clearly, from the progressive reformists in the Lower House — the Makabayan bloc, Gabriela, ACT Teachers, Kabataan, people like France Castro, Arlene Brosas, and Raoul Manuel?

I’ve always been deeply curious about the story of Fr. Juan Severino Mallari. 

I wanted to understand how a priest could end up remembered as the first serial killer in the Philippines.

When I sit with the scattered accounts long enough, the glow of 2025 fades, and Magalang in the early 1800s comes into focus.

The town lies under the shadow of Mount Arayat, rice fields flattening out in every direction, the air heavy with mud, smoke, and the sweet-sour smell of carabao. 

At its center stands San Bartolome Church: thick stone walls, a simple façade, the kind of structure that has already survived more than one earthquake. 

Inside, candles bruise the darkness in small, wavering circles. 

A young priest bends over a desk in the rectory, his hand moving in slow, deliberate strokes across parchment.

Ink flowers bloom in the margins of parish reports—roses and vines curling around columns of names and numbers, angels perched on clouds above lists of baptisms and burials. 

His calligraphy is so precise it looks printed, each letter disciplined yet somehow alive. 

At the bottom of each page, beneath the flourishes, he signs his name: Juan Severino Mallari.

Just reaching this desk was a kind of battle. 

Born in Macabebe, he studied theology at the University of Santo Tomas, one of the first few Filipinos to step through doors meant for Spanish sons. 

He applied three times for parish assignments—Orani, Mariveles, Lubao—and three times the colonial authorities chose Spaniards instead. 

Filipino priests, they said in different words, were not ready. 

They were too provincial, too superstitious, too indio. 

When he finally secured the parish of Magalang in 1816, he became the first native priest to hold a parish in Pampanga, a single brown face in a clergy dominated by white men.

On Sundays, he moves through the liturgy with practiced grace. 

The people watch him at the altar—this priest who speaks their language and carries their features, yet wears the vestments of a power that has ruled them for centuries. 

They come to him with all the usual needs: a sick child, a cramped marriage, a bad harvest. He listens, prays, writes their names into his neat columns.

Then his mother arrives.

She comes from Macabebe to live with him, already showing signs of a strange illness. 

At first it looks like exhaustion: a little more sleep, a little less appetite. 

Then it grips harder. Fever, weakness, weight dropping off her frame, the body shrinking into itself. 

There is no clear diagnosis in the books he read in Manila, no Latin label for this kind of wasting. Doctors are few, and their remedies are limited. Herbal healers try their concoctions and fail.

Around them, the town supplies a different explanation, older than the church walls: nakulam. Someone has cursed her. 

In the stalls of the market and the corners of nipa houses, people murmur about mangkukulam—those who can send sickness across distance with herbs, prayers, and pins stuck into wax or cloth. 

Catholicism has not erased this vocabulary; it has learned to live beside it. A person can recite the Creed and still fear a curse.

It is not hard to imagine how this word lands on a mind under strain. 

Mallari is the first Filipino priest in a system that has told him, over and over, that he does not belong. 

The pressure to be perfect presses on him from all sides: from Spanish superiors waiting for him to fail, from parishioners expecting one of their own to be better than the friars, from his own ambition and insecurity. 

Now, in the close dark of his quarters, the person he loves most lies dying, and nobody can explain why.

He prays harder. He clutches the rosary until his fingers ache. In the silence between beads, he starts to hear another kind of logic: if someone cast a curse, someone can undo it. 

If witches did this, witches must be stopped. 

Saving his mother becomes not only a plea to God but a task, a mission that begins to form in the cracks of his sanity.

The first body appears sometime after 1816. 

The records will not give the victim’s name—only that people in Magalang began to disappear or to turn up dead without clear cause. 

A farmer found in a field. 
A woman who doesn’t make it home. 
A laborer whose death no one can quite explain.

 Each case looks, at first glance, like an isolated tragedy in a province where hardship is normal and native deaths are low priorities for colonial officials.

He continues to say Mass.

In the rectory, people keep coming. 

A man wanting counsel about a family dispute; a woman seeking prayers for a sick child; a parishioner with a small favor to ask. They enter the priest’s private space, trusting the collar and the gentle voice they hear every Sunday. 

Behind that door, something else is happening. 

In the fractured map of his mind, they are not neighbors but suspects, not sheep to be shepherded but possible sources of the curse eating his mother alive.

The exact details of each killing are lost. There is no surviving transcript describing how he chose, what he said, how he moved. 

What remains are signs. 

Over the years, more townspeople vanish. Some are found dead with no obvious motive or thief to blame. 

The pattern is invisible to those looking only at the surface. It will only become clear when someone finally sees what he has been hiding.

His mother dies on December 4, 1825. 

Whatever bargain he believed he was keeping, whatever ritual he thought he was fulfilling, fails in the most obvious way. Her body cools. Her breath stops. The curse, if there ever was one, has run its course.

The killings do not stop.

By this point, whatever line once separated his grief from his delusion has blurred beyond recovery. He has been living for years in a mental world where invisible enemies surround him, where each death is not murder but necessary medicine. 

Even without his original motive, the logic of that world holds. The idea of witches does not vanish with his mother’s last breath. It has sunk too deep.

In December 1826, Mallari falls ill himself. 

He can no longer perform all his duties, and another priest is sent to assist and, if needed, take over the running of the parish. 

This newcomer walks into the same stone rectory, smells the same faint mix of candle wax and old paper, and begins to learn the daily rhythms of the parish. 

He also begins to open drawers and chests in search of linens, vestments, anything required for the sacraments.

Somewhere in that search, a lid lifts on the hidden evidence.

Inside a chest or cabinet—again, the specifics are not preserved—he finds clothes. 

Many clothes. 

Men’s shirts, women’s skirts, children’s garments. 

They are neatly folded, but the neatness cannot hide the stains: brown-black patches where blood dried long ago. 

They are not his. They do not belong to any living resident of the rectory. They belong to people who have gone missing, to bodies found without explanation, to families who never received these last traces back.

This is the point where the story stops being rumor and becomes a case.

Authorities are notified. 
Soldiers arrive. 
Mallari is arrested, taken from the rectory where his mother once lay and his ink once dried. 

In questioning, according to later accounts, he does not deny what he has done. Instead, he explains. These were witches. They had cursed his mother. He killed them to break the spell. He believes, or at least once believed, that he was fighting evil with evil, blood with blood.

For the Spanish colonial authorities, his confession is useful. 

It confirms what they have long whispered: native priests are unstable, too close to the “superstitions” of the people, unfit for the responsibilities they crave. 

In a Church already torn by tensions over secularization—the question of whether Filipino secular clergy should be given more parishes instead of the powerful religious orders—Mallari’s case becomes an argument in flesh and rope for keeping Filipinos out.

The law, such as it is, moves slowly. Mallari is taken to Manila and imprisoned. 

Fourteen years pass between his arrest and his execution. 

In those years, another institution stands across the city: Hospicio de San Jose, an island refuge on the Pasig (side note: the river, not the place) where orphans, the elderly, and the mentally ill are taken in and cared for by religious sisters. 

The place is hardly a modern psychiatric hospital, but it represents a different possibility: that those whose minds have broken might be contained and tended rather than displayed and killed.

Mallari does not go there. He remains in prison, his case winding through a colonial legal system more interested in example than in understanding. 

Whatever diagnosis a modern psychiatrist might give—psychosis, severe mental disorder—will only be applied retroactively by later historians. At the time, there is only crime and punishment.

By 1840, the long wait ends.

Bagumbayan, the wide field outside the walls of Intramuros, is bare under the Manila sky. It serves as a military parade ground, a public space, and a stage for executions. 

The gallows are visible from a distance. People gather, some out of morbid curiosity, some because any public spectacle breaks the monotony of colonial life.

Guards bring him out. He has been in custody for more than a decade. His hair, his face, his posture are all altered by years in confinement that the records do not bother to describe. They climb the scaffold. 

One Filipino priest about to be hanged for murdering dozens of his own people.

No document records his final thoughts.

No scribe writes down his last words in detail. 

The rope is placed around his neck. 

Whatever he believed about witches, curses, his mother, and God must resolve into something in that instant, but history keeps it to itself. 

The trap gives way. 

The crowd watches. 

The colonial authorities show that even a priest can hang.

Decades later, Bagumbayan will claim other clerical bodies: Fathers Gomez, Burgos, and Zamora, executed in 1872 after a rebellion they did not lead, their deaths fueling a secularization movement and nationalist fire. 

José Rizal will fall there in 1896, facing a firing squad with his back turned. 

Their names are etched into monuments and textbooks. Mallari’s name is mostly an oddity in crime lists and horror articles.

His victims, estimated at fifty-seven, remain largely faceless and nameless in the surviving accounts. 

Their families do not appear in the official record. 

The number itself is repeated so often it feels solid, yet when traced back it rests on thin documentation, on partial reports and later reconstructions. 

What is certain is that people died, that they were parishioners who trusted their priest, and that their stories were never given the weight his has received.

Today, San Bartolome Church still stands in Magalang. 

Its stones have been repaired and repainted. Its bells still ring over the town. 

Families gather there for Mass and weddings and funerals, most of them not thinking about the first Filipino priest who once wrote their ancestors’ names in elegant script and hid their murdered neighbors’ clothes in his quarters. 

If you sit quietly in a pew long enough, the building feels more like a survivor than a haunted house—a place that has watched generations pass and kept its own counsel.

The story of Fr. Juan Severino Mallari hangs between categories that modern readers like to keep separate. 

He is a killer, yes, a man who took lives in numbers that stagger for a rural parish. 

He is also, by all reasonable inferences, a man whose mind broke under the combined weight of personal grief, cultural beliefs about curses, and a colonial system that gave him responsibility but little support and no language for mental illness. 

He is a priest elevated as an exception and then paraded as a warning.

There is no neat moral to extract. No redemption arc, no clean condemnation that does justice to both the horror of what he did and the context that shaped him. 

What we have instead are fragments: calligraphed reports, later testimonies, numbers without names, a stretch of ground in Manila where his body once swung, a church in Pampanga that goes on ringing its bells.

The rest happens in the space between the reader and the page, where imagination tries to fill the gaps that history left behind.

Hi MCT. Curious lang ako – why does your writing look ChatGPT?
Part of what makes it look like an AI generated content are the red square emojis you are using.
Thanks.
– Jimmy

Hi Jimmy. Thanks for your email.

The red square emoji you’re talking about is actually a solution to a problem I had, back when I still had control over MCT 1.0.

If you’ve been following MCT for some time, you might remember that long post where I complained about Facebook’s lack of proper blog formatting. Facebook was never meant for long-form writing, and it shows.

The obvious option was to post everything on www.morningcoffeethoughts.org.

But here’s the catch.
Hostinger, my website host and web designer, doesn’t have an Android app. That means I can’t post on the fly. No quick edits. No spontaneous publishing.

So I looked for a workaround.

I asked ChatGPT what I could do since Facebook doesn’t support real headings. The suggestion was simple. Use a visual marker for headings. A square block.

Why red?

First, red is the MCT brand color.
Second, I have a color perception problem. I only clearly see a limited range of colors. Red, blue, and green. Red stands out the most. 

That’s the reason behind the red squares. Structure, readability, and accessibility. Nothing more.

Now, just to be clear.

Yes, I do use ChatGPT.
But not for writing.

I mostly use it to help format text so posts are easier to read.

I also have a love-hate relationship with ChatGPT. Part of that is personal. I lost 12 clients because of it.

Still, the tool is convenient, especially when I’m dealing with large amounts of data and trying to make sense of everything in my head.

But when it comes to writing, I have one rule.

My words.
My thoughts.
My ideas.

I don’t let ChatGPT write for me. Because like I said, my audience is myself. And if I let something else do the thinking and writing for me, para ko na ring niloko ang sarili ko.

For context, I’m a professional blogger. Between my own work and former clients, I’ve written a little over 5,000 blogs. Blogging paid the bills for years. It was my livelihood.

Then ChatGPT arrived, and I lost all my clients.

With my research and writing skills sitting idle, I decided to put them to use somewhere else.

That’s where Morning Coffee Thoughts comes in.

MCT was never meant to be a political page. I have always said I am not an analyst. I am not a pundit. Just a blogger expressing his thoughts while having coffee. Honestly, I was surprised people even started following it.

The reasons behind MCT are personal.

One, I was diagnosed with an early form of dementia. My doctor suggested I find an activity that could help sharpen my memory and slow its effects.

Second, I wanted to leave something behind for my daughter, just in case I’m no longer around.

Third, I’m 52 now and I don’t really have a personal legacy to leave behind.

And lastly, social media became too noisy. Fake news everywhere. Figuring out what’s true and what isn’t started to feel exhausting.

So MCT became my way of taking control of my learning.
For lack of a better word, it’s my thinking space.

I didn’t design MCT to convince anyone.
Its primary audience is me.

Para kapag dumating ang 2028, malinaw sa isip ko kung sino ang dapat kong iboto.

MCT isn’t here to impress anyone.
It’s here to help me remember who I am, what I believe, and why.

If it helps others, that’s a bonus.
If not, that’s okay too.
